headline: Apple hikes prices as memory chip costs skyrocket
summary:
STORY: :: Apple

Apple is hiking prices as the cost of memory chips skyrockets.

Its lowest priced laptop will jump from $599 to $699, with iPad prices also rising.

For now at least, the iPhone is unaffected.

Apple says it can no longer avoid passing on rising costs to consumers.

The price of memory chips jumped by around 98% in the first quarter, and has continued to soar ever since.

That is driven by the boom in AI data centers.

Companies like Nvidia have signed big deals with chipmakers, leaving consumer electronics firms scrambling to secure supplies.

Apple said it had never seen component prices increase so much, so quickly.

While iPhones weren’t hit by Thursday’s move, analysts say it’s only a matter of time before they too face price increases.

Apple shares fell almost 5%, while Dell dropped 8%.

Some industry watchers say Apple’s rivals may have to do even bigger price increases, as they lack the Mac maker’s deep ties to suppliers.

Research firm IDC says the global smartphone and computer markets will both see sharp contractions this year due to the rising prices.

headline: The Week in Numbers: Micron climbs high, Apple raises prices
summary:
STORY: From Micron's mega market cap to Apple's price hikes...

...this is the Week in Numbers.

:: The Week in Numbers

:: $1.4 trillion.

About $1.4 trillion was Micron's market cap after it briefly blew past the value of Meta and Tesla for the first time.

It came after the firm forecast quarterly profit and revenue well above expectations.

Micron is a major memory-chip maker and a key supplier to AI titan Nvidia.

The company's shares have more than tripled in value so far this year, and soared close to a fifth following its results.

:: 5%

Almost 5% is how far Apple shares fell after it announced price hikes.

The tech giant said its cheapest laptop will jump from $599 to $699, with iPad prices also rising - although the iPhone won't jump for now.

Apple says it can no longer avoid passing on rising costs to consumers.

The price of memory chips jumped by around 98% in the first quarter, and has kept soaring due to the AI boom.

:: 42% 

42% was how much shares of Wendy's soared as retail traders bought up the beaten-down stock in the latest meme-like rally.

Trading on the heavily shorted stock was paused multiple times due to volatility..

Shares briefly hit a more than seven-month high.

The fast food chain is battling weak sales and pressure from an activist investor, with its stock down more than 78% from record highs five years ago.

:: $648 billion

Almost $648 billion is how much Samsung plans to invest in South Korea over 10 years.

That's according to the Maeil Business Newspaper, which said the firm will announce its intentions on Monday.

The investment includes a potential $194 billion to build chip factories in the country's southwest, as well as plans for AI data centers, batteries and displays.

There was no immediate response to a request for comment from Samsung.

:: $606.5 million

And $606.5 million was H&M's operating profit during its fiscal second quarter.

That was smaller than expected for the Swedish fashion retailer.

H&M blamed weaker consumer demand and inflation made worse by the Iran war.

CEO Daniel Erver said Europe, particularly the western part of it, had gone through a difficult quarter.

headline: HIVE Digital upsizes note to $115m to fuel AI and GPU cloud expansion
summary: HIVE Digital Technologies Ltd (TSX:HIVE, NASDAQ:HIVE, FRA:YO0, BVC:HIVECO) CEO Aydin Kilic joined Proactive's Stephen Gunnion to discuss the company's upsized US$115 million zero-coupon exchangeable note offering and how it will accelerate HIVE's AI infrastructure, GPU cloud and HPC ambitions. Kilic explained that the financing, originally launched at US$100 million before being upsized, offers a low-cost source of capital to support HIVE's scaling strategy, with room for further expansion. He pointed to the company's recent agreement with Cohere involving around 2,300 NVIDIA GPUs, plus another significant GPU opportunity in the pipeline, with proceeds earmarked to help finance future GPU acquisitions. The discussion also covered HIVE's Boden, Sweden facility, where municipal approval to purchase the land marks a major milestone in converting the site into a Tier III HPC data centre. Kilic said the financing will help fund the long-lead equipment needed for that upgrade. On demand, Kilic said: "People always value time and money, and that applies in the world of AI and compute. Everybody wants compute now." He also highlighted the appeal of long-term HPC contracts over the cyclical nature of crypto mining, pointing to the potential for stable cash flows and long-term value creation.
